coredata.dc:title: Sulfur and Nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dot decorated ZnO nanorod/polymer hybrid flexible device for photosensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Hmar, Jehova Jire L.
Majumder, Tanmoy
Dhar, Saurab
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal process. S,N-GQDs are made up of 1–5 monolayer of graphene with average diameter 13.3nm. The absorption peaks at 336 and 621nm, are attributed to n→П⁎ transitions of electrons in CO and SO bonds, respectively. S,N-GQDs are highly luminescent and showed excitation dependent emission behaviors. Hybrid photosensing device has been fabricated with S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods and a conjugated polymer poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT). S,N-GQD decorated ZnO nanorod demonstrated higher photoresponse compared to pristine ZnO nanorod based device. S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorod hybrid device showed superior incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE), photoresponsivity and detectivity compared to the control samples. The flexibility study of the samples has been monitored by measuring current-voltage characteristics at different bending angles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Nitrogen
Sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs)
ZnO nanorods
Photoresponse
Incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE)
Photoresponsivity and detectivity

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Thin Film Devices, Sensors and Actuators

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: (i) Synthesis of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs)
Microstructure and optical properties of S,N-GQDs
Characterizations of ZnO NRs and S,N-GQD sensitized NRs
Electrical properties of hybrid nanocomposite devices
Flexibility study of S,N-GQDs/ZnO/P3HT hybrid nanocomposites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This present research work was funded by CSIR Extramural Research Scheme , Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledge the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for UV–VIS-NIR and AFM characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation for the growth of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots. (b) Schematic representation for the growth of ZnO nanorods on ITO coated flexible PET substrates.
Schematic representation for the hybrid device fabrication on patterned ITO coated flexible PET substrates.

(a) Atomic force microscopy image of S,N-doped GQDs dispersed on p-Si substrate. (b) Height distribution plot of S,N-doped GQDs. (c) Plane view TEM micrograph of hydrothermally grown S,N-GQDs. (d) Size distribution plot of S,N-GQDs.
(a) Typical XPS survey scan spectrum of S,N-GQDs. The high resolution XPS spectra of S, N:GQDs (b) S2p peak and (c) N 1s peak.

(a) Typical UV-visible absorption spectrum of S, N-GQDs. (b) Photoluminescence spectra of S,N-GQDs under different excitation wavelength in the range of 360–460nm. Photograph of S,N-GQDs solutions under sunlight and 360nm UV excitation (inset).

SEM photographs of ZnO nanorods grown on ITO coated flexible PET substrates (a) top view and (b) tilted view of ZnO nanorods. ZnO nanorod diameter size distribution histogram is plotted at upper inset.

(a) TEM micrograph of S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorods after detachment from the substrates. (b) Magnified view of S,N-GQDs/ZnO nanorods. The attachments of S,N-GQDs are indicated by circles. (c) High resolution TEM image of ZnO nanorods. (d) SAED pattern of ZnO nanorods.
X-ray diffraction spectra of ZnO nanorods and S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorods grown on PET substrates.
UV-visible absorption spectra of ZnO nanorods before and after attachment of S,N-GQDs. (αhν)2 vs hν plot for pristine ZnO NRs and S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs(inset).
Raman spectra of ZnO nanorods and S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorod samples.

(a) J-V characteristics of device S1 under dark and illumination of white light of intensity 80mW/cm2. J-V characteristics of the same device under dark condition (inset). (b) J-V characteristics of device S1, S2 and S3 under illumination of white light of intensity 80mW/cm2.
(a) Photoresponse behaviors of device S1, S2 and S3 at zero bias. (b) Photoresponse behaviors of device S1 at different bias. The intensity of illuminated broad band light source is 80mW/cm2.
(a) IPCE (%) vs wavelength plot of device S1, S2 and S3. (b) Schematic energy band diagram and electron-hole transport of S1 device.
Spectral response of (a) photoresponsivity and (b) photodetectivity of device S1 at different bias (0 to −5V).

J-V characteristics of S1 device grown on patterned ITO coated flexible PET substrates under dark condition. J-V characteristics were recorded at 180° (without bending), 50°, 90° and 120° bending angles of the substrates. Photograph demonstrates the flexibility of a typical S1 device (inset).

head.ce:title: Sulfur and Nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dot decorated ZnO nanorod/polymer hybrid flexible device for photosensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) have been synthesized by a hydrothermal process. S,N-GQDs are made up of 1–5 monolayer of graphene with average diameter 13.3nm. The absorption peaks at 336 and 621nm, are attributed to n→П⁎ transitions of electrons in CO and SO bonds, respectively. S,N-GQDs are highly luminescent and showed excitation dependent emission behaviors. Hybrid photosensing device has been fabricated with S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO nanorods and a conjugated polymer poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT). S,N-GQD decorated ZnO nanorod demonstrated higher photoresponse compared to pristine ZnO nanorod based device. S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorod hybrid device showed superior incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE), photoresponsivity and detectivity compared to the control samples. The flexibility study of the samples has been monitored by measuring current-voltage characteristics at different bending angles.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE)

ce:section.ce:para: ZnO is an important n-type semiconductor, has been studied extensively due to its wide direct band gap (~3.2–3.4eV), high electron mobility, nontoxicity, higher excitonic binding energy (60meV), and environment friendly nature [1–4]. Recently, one dimensional (1D) ZnO nanostructures such as nanorods [5], nanowires [6] and nanotubes [7] have attracted much attention for their potential applications in optoelectronic devices such as nanowire lasers [8], photodetectors [9], field emitters [10], light-emitting diodes [11] and solar cells [12]. Attachment of semiconductors quantum dots (QDs) improves photoabsorption, charge separation and charge transport ability of ZnO nanorods due to their favorable band alignment and strong photoabsorption in visible range [13–15]. There are plenty of reports on sensitization of ZnO nanorods and nanowires with chalcogenide semiconductor QDs to improve the photovoltaic properties, and optoelectronic behaviors [15–17]. However, most of these chalcogenide semiconductor QDs are toxic and not environment friendly. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention because of their size dependent optical and electrical properties and possible applications in solar cell [18,19], bioimaging [20], dye-sensitized solar cells [21], photocatalyst [22] and photoelectrochemical solar cells [23,24] etc. The electronic and optical properties of these zero dimensional nanomaterials can be tailored by doping with other elements like S, N, B, Si etc. [24–27]. Sensitization of ZnO nanorods with GQDs could be attractive due to their low cost, nontoxic nature and strong photoabsorption in uv–visible region depending on their doping elements. Yang et al. [28] studied GQD decorated ZnO nanorods grown on interdigital Au electrodes. The optoelectronic behaviors of GQDs coated nanorods under UV light illumination were investigated. The superior photocurrent of sensitized nanorods was attributed to the interfacial charge transfer from GQDs to ZnO. Zhang et al. [29] studied CQD coated TiO2 nanotubes for photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation. The photocurrent density of CQD/TiO2 is found to be four times higher than pristine TiO2 nanotubes. Majumder et al. [24,30] demonstrated the advantages of N doped GQD as a photosensitizer with ZnO nanorods.

Recently, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) are studied due to their broad photoabsorption in wide spectral range, high carrier transport mobility and excellent chemical stability [31,32]. Sensitization of high band gap semiconductors like ZnO/TiO2 with S,N GQDs could be attractive for photoabsorption in broad spectral region. Various methods have been demonstrated for the preparation of S,N-GQDs, such as electrochemical oxidation processes [33], microwave synthesis [34], chemical vapor deposition [35] and hydrothermal synthesis [35,36].

Here, we report, the synthesis of S, N co-doped GQDs using a hydrothermal method [31]. These S,N-GQDs were attached with ZnO nanorods (ZnO NRs). ZnO NRs were grown hydrothermally on indium tin oxide (ITO) coated flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrates. The photosensing behaviors of S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO NRs/conducting polymer poly(3 hexylthiophene) (P3HT) hybrid devices have been studied in details.

ce:section.ce:section-title: (i) Synthesis of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The details synthesis process of S and N co-doped GQDs has been reported elsewhere [31]. In typical experiment, 1.26g (1mmol) citric acid and 1.38g (3mmol) thiourea were dissolved in 30ml of de-ionized (DI) water and stirred for several minutes to obtain a clear solution. The solution was transferred in a 50ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated up to constant 180°C for 8h and cooled down to room temperature and kept for additional 12h for further use. The formation mechanism of GQD in hydrothermal process using citric acid as the carbon precursor has been reported by Qu et al. [31]. At first, citric acid molecules are self-assembled and nanosheet structures are formed due the inter-molecular H-bonding. After dehydrolysis process, graphene quantum dots are produced along with several carboxyl- and hydroxyl-groups. During hydrothermal process, with the reaction of thiourea molecules, N and S atoms are covalently bonded with carbon atoms and S, N co-doped GQD are synthesized [24,37]. The schematic representation of the formation mechanism of S,N-GQD is presented in Fig. 1(a). The positions of S and N atoms in GQD has been determined by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study and discussed in later section.

The growth of ZnO nanorods was carried out by a hydrothermal method, which has been reported in earlier literatures [36,38]. In brief, a thin seed layer of ZnO nanoparticles was deposited on patterned ITO coated flexible PET substrates by spin coating process. To make ZnO nanoparticle solutions, at first 0.01M of zinc acetate (Merck, 99%) was dissolved in 2-propanal (Merck, 99%) followed by heating and stirring at 65°C. After 30min, diethanolamine (Nice, 99%) was added to the previous solution, and a transparent solution was achieved. ZnO nanoparticle seed layers were deposited from the previous solution by spin coating on patterned ITO coated PET substrates with a spin speed 2000rpm for 60s. The hydrothermal growth was carried out in a sealed borosil glass bottle by immersing the seeded substrates vertically in a solution containing zinc nitrate (SDFCL, 98%) and hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) (SDFCL, 99%). The reaction temperature was maintained at 95°C for 4h. To attach S,N-GQDs on ZnO nanorod surface, PET substrates were dipped in S,N-GQDs solution for 2h. The samples were dried at 90°C in air for 30min to remove the solvents. The growth of ZnO NRs is schematically shown in Fig. 1(b).

Inorganic-organic hybrid composite devices have been fabricated using P3HT as a conducting polymer. At first, polymer solution was prepared by dissolving regioregular solid P3HT powder (Sigma Aldrich) at a concentration of 15mg/ml in chloroform solvent by stirring overnight. The P3HT solutions were spin-coated on S,N-GQDs decorated ZnO nanorods with a spin speed of 1500rpm for 60s. The samples were heated at 90°C for 30min to remove the solvent. A thin hole conducting polymer, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) (Sigma Aldrich) layer was spin coated on composite devices with spin speed 1500rpm. Electrical contacts were made from bottom ITO electrodes and top Al electrodes, which were thermally evaporated on PEDOT:PSS film. The active electrode area of the samples was 8×10−6 m2. The schematic representation of various steps for device fabrication is presented in Fig. 2 .

The surface morphology of S,N-GQDs, ZnO seed layers and ZnO nanorods were carried out by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker, MultiMode-8) in tapping mode. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements of GQDs and ZnO NRs were carried out using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope with an accelerating voltage 200kV. The chemical bonding of S,N-GQDs were measured using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe II (ULVAC-PHI, INC, Japan) x-ray photoelectron spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα x-ray source of energy 1486.6eV. The field-emission scanning electron micrograph (FESEM) images of ZnO NRs were recorded using a ZEISS SUPRA 40 system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured by a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer with CuKα (λ=0.15418nm) radiation operated at 45kV and 40mA. The optical absorption spectra of the samples were recorded in the wavelength range 300–800nm using a UV–VIS–NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950). Current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the devices were studied using a source meter (B2912A, Agilent, USA) under dark and illumination of broad band light source [Sciencetech Inc., Canada]. The spectral dependent photocurrent response characteristics were measured using an assembled setup consisting of a broad band light source, monochromator [Sciencetech Inc., Canada], mechanical chopper and lock-in amplifier [Stanford Research, SR 530].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and optical properties of S,N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface topography and average height of S,N-GQDs were studied by atomic force microscopy in tapping mode. A drop of S,N-GQDs solution was dispersed on SiO2 substrate for AFM characterizations. Fig. 3(a) shows the typical AFM micrograph of S and N doped graphene quantum dots. The height distribution plot of GQDs is represented in Fig. 3(b). The histogram clearly indicates that, the heights of GQDs are mostly distributed in the range of 0.5–2nm. This observation suggests that S,N-GQDs are consisted of 1–5 monolayers of graphene and more than 72% of GQDs are comprised of 1–3 layers [31,39]. Fig. 3(c) shows the typical plane view TEM micrograph of S,N-GQDs. The size distribution plot is presented in Fig. 3(d). The average size estimated from TEM micrograph is found to be 13.3nm.

Fig. 4(a) represents, the full range XPS spectrum of S,N-GQDs deposited on silicon substrates. The binding energy peaks of C1s at 284eV, N 1s at 400eV, S2p at 168eV and O 1s at 533eV confirmed the presence of S and N doping in GQDs [31,40]. The peaks of Si 2s at 150eV and Si 2p at 99eV are observed from silicon substrates. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p and N 1s electron are plotted in Fig. 4b and Fig. 4c, respectively. The high resolution peak of S 2p electron has been deconvoluted into three Gaussian parts. The peaks located at 163.3eV, 164.2eV and 168.1eV are attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene and SO bonding, respectively [40,41]. On the other hand, high resolution N 1s electronic peak has been fitted by two major peaks. The peaks at 399.4eV and 401.3eV are accredited to the pyrrolic and graphitic N bands, respectively [40].

Fig. 5(a) shows the typical UV–visible absorption spectrum of S,N-GQDs. The absorption spectrum shows two peaks, one strong peak at 337nm, which is similar to the hydrothermally grown graphene quantum dots and another broad absorption band at 618nm. The origin of absorption peak at 337nm is attributed n→П⁎ electron transition in CO groups [31]. On the other hand, the broad absorption band at 618nm arises due to the presence of sulfur doping and originated from the transition of n→П⁎ electrons in SO bonds [31].The excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence spectra of S,N-GQDs are presented in Fig. 5(b). It has been observed that, S,N-GQDs are highly luminescent. Under sunlight, the solution color is yellow as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(b). However, after illumination with UV radiation (360nm) it emits blue light ( ̴ 437nm) (inset of Fig. 5b). The PL excitation wavelengths were varied from 360–460nm. Interestingly, as the excitation wavelength increases, the PL peak position red shifted from 440 to 543nm and the intensity of emission peak decreases, which is similar to the reported results [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of ZnO NRs and S,N-GQD sensitized NRs
ce:section.ce:para: SEM micrographs of ZnO nanorods are shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b). Fig. 6(a) shows the top view image of ZnO nanorods grown on PET substrates. The micrograph clearly indicates the formation of ZnO nanorods uniformly distributed in large area. The diameter size distribution of the ZnO nanorods is presented at the inset of Fig. 6(a). It has been observed that, the diameter of ZnO nanorods ranges from 75 to 356nm and the average diameter of the nanorods is found to be 250nm. ZnO NRs are highly dense and uniformly distributed over the entire sample. The average nanorod density has been estimated from 6μm×4μm surface area and found to be ~2.3×1013 rods/m2.

Fig. 7(a) shows the plane view TEM micrograph of S,N-GQD/ZnO nanorods. The average length and diameter of the nanorods has been estimated from TEM micrograph and found to be 500nm and 110nm, respectively. The magnified image of ZnO NRs (Fig. 7b) shows several tiny circular dots (indicated by circles) correspond to S, N GQD attachment on ZnO NRs surface. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph of ZnO NRs is represented in Fig. 7(c). It confirmed the formation of single crystalline wurtzite ZnO nanorods grown along (002) direction with lattice spacing 0.26nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 7d) also confirmed the formation of single crystalline ZnO NRs along (002) growth direction.

X-ray diffraction measurements were carried out to identify the crystalline behaviors of ZnO and S-N-GQD/ZnO NRs grown on ITO coated flexible PET substrates. Fig. 8 shows the XRD spectra of ZnO NRs before and after sensitization with S,N-GQDs. The XRD result clearly indicates that, our as grown ZnO nanorods are highly crystalline. The dominant diffraction peaks at (100), (002), (101), (102) and (110) planes are attributed to the hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO, with lattice parameters a=b=3.264Å and c=5.219Å (JCPDS File Card 79-0208). It can be mentioned that, no significant change has been observed in XRD pattern after attachment of S,N-GQDs. However, the additional diffraction peaks at 26° and 52.9° have been observed due to the diffraction from PET substrate [42].
Fig. 9 shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnO nanorods before and after attachment of S,N-GQDs.

The optical band gap (E
g ) of ZnO NRs has been calculated using Tauc's formula [43].
(1)
αhν
2
= A
hν −
E g
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the incident photon energy and A is a constant. Inset of Fig. 9 represents (αhν)2 vs hν plot for both samples. The optical band gap of ZnO NRs and absorption edge of S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs have been calculated by extrapolation at the linear region of (αhν)2 vs hν plot to the energy axis. The pristine ZnO NRs shows strong absorption in the UV region of wavelength threshold at 387nm, corresponding to the energy band gap of 3.2eV. Interestingly, the absorption edge has been shifted significantly to higher wavelength for S,N-GQD decorated NRs (2.85eV), which is attributed to the strengthened photoabsorption in visible region [44].

The attachment of S,N-GQDs on ZnO NRs was also confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 10 shows the Raman spectra of ZnO NRs and S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs samples. Both samples exhibit strong characteristic vibrational modes of ZnO at 336.2cm−1 and 440.2cm−1. An additional broad vibrational band at 600cm−1 also observed for both samples, which is ascribed to the oxygen related vacancies in ZnO [45]. However, S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs sample shows two extra peaks at 1340 and 1600cm−1 , which are attributed to the strong graphite (G) and defect (D) bands of carbon atoms in S,N-GQDs, respectively [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of hybrid nanocomposite devices
ce:section.ce:para: To study the advantages of S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO NRs for photosensing applications, the following three samples have been fabricated. The samples are (i) ITO/S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO NRs/P3HT/PEDOT:PSS/Al (denoted as S1) (ii) ITO/ZnO NRs/P3HT/PEDOT:PSS/Al (denoted as S2) and (iii) ITO/P3HT/PEDOT:PSS/Al (denoted as S3). Fig. 11(a) shows the J-V characteristics of S1 under the dark and illumination of broad band light source (80mW/cm2). After illumination, the current density increases due to the photoexcited carrier generation in the hybrid device. As for example, the current density measured at 1V under dark and illumination of white light are 2.6mA/cm2 and 307.2mA/cm2, respectively. Fig. 11(b) shows the J-V characteristics of the composite device (S1) and the control samples (S2 & S3) with light. Interestingly, the current density value for S1 increases 4 times than S2 and 7 times than S3 at 1V bias voltage.

Fig. 12(a) shows the photosensing behaviors of S1 device and the control samples (S2&S3). The photocurrent was measured without external bias under illumination of broadband light of intensity 80mW/cm2. Interestingly, S1 device demonstrated superior photoresponse in comparison with other control samples. The on/off ratio for S1, S2 and S3 samples are 1.3×103, 0.8×103 and 0.7×103, respectively. The photoresponse behaviors of S1 device at different bias is presented in Fig. 12(b). The on/off ratio at bias voltage 0, −1, −2, −4 and −5V are 1.3×103, 1.7×103, 1.9×103 and 2.1×103, respectively.

To measure the spectral photoresponse behaviors, incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements for all samples have been carried out. The IPCE values have been calculated using the following relation [47]
(2)
IPCE
%
=
1240
J λ
λ
P λ
× 100
where J λ is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2) , P λ is the light intensity of the incident light (mW/cm2) at a particular wavelength λ. Fig. 13(a) shows variation of IPCE with incident wavelength varied from 300–700nm for all samples. The maximum IPCE (%) value of S2 sample is 11% at 360nm and S3 is 3.1% at 640nm. However, after attachment of S,N-GQDs with ZnO NRs, it enhances over broad wavelength range (300–700nm) compared to the control samples and attained the maximum value 18.7% at 360nm. The superior photoconductive nature and higher IPCE value of GQDs sensitized nanorod based device could be explained with the help of energy band diagram. The carrier transport mechanism of S1 device is schematically shown in Fig. 13(b). The reported values of work function for ITO, ZnO, P3HT, PEDOT:PSS and Al have been used in the proposed band diagram [48,49]. Under the illumination of white light, electron-hole pairs are generated in the hybrid device and transported to the electrodes as shown in the Fig. 13(b). The attachment of S,N-GQDs with ZnO NRs improves the photoabsorption in UV as well as in visible region, which improves the photocurrent and IPCE value of the device. In UV–visible absorption study (Fig. 9), S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs showed higher absorbance (absorption coefficient) compared to pristine ZnO NRs, due to the strong photoabsorption in the wavelength range 330–500nm. This corroborates our photocurrent and IPCE study. Attachment of S,N-GQDs also create favorable band alignments in the hybrid device, which results efficient electron-hole transfer at the electrodes under illumination of light.

For photodetector applications, wavelength dependent photoresponsivity and photodetectivity have been measured at bias voltage 0, −1, −2, −4 and −5V. The photoresponsivity (R) has been measured corresponding to a particular wavelength using the following equation [50]:
(3)
R
λ
=
J λ
P λ
where J λ is the photocurrent density and P λ is the intensity of incident light at a wavelength λ. Fig. 14 (a) shows the wavelength dependent photoresponsivity of S1 device over the spectral range 300nm–700nm for different reverse bias voltage (0V, −2V, −4V, and −5V). The maximum responsivity has been found at λ=360nm for all bias voltage. The photoresponsivity significantly enhances with increase in sample bias. As for example, the values of R at bias 0, −2, −4 and −5V are found to be 0.05, 0.06, 0.07 and 0.08A/W respectively.

The photodetectivity the samples have been calculated using the relation [50]
(4)
D =
R
λ
2 q
J d
where q is the charge of electron and J
d is the dark current density.

The photodetectivity plot with wavelength (300–700nm) at various bias voltage for device S1 is shown in Fig. 14(b). Similar to responsivity curve, the photodetectivity also increases with sample bias and a maximum value is obtained at wavelength λ=360nm. The maximum detectivity of the device is found to be 2.3×1010, 2.8×1010, 3.1×1010, and 3.5×1010 cmHz1/2/W at sample bias 0V, −2V, −4V, and −5V, respectively. The enhancement of IPCE, photoresponsivity and photodetectivity at λ=360nm is attributed to strong UV absorption of S,N-GQDs as well as ZnO nanorods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flexibility study of S,N-GQDs/ZnO/P3HT hybrid nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In this present study, we have also demonstrated the flexibility test of S1 hybrid device. Fig. 15 represents the J-V characteristics of hybrid device without and with bending of angle 50°, 90° and 120° under dark condition. The photograph of hybrid device with bending is presented at the inset of Fig. 15. Interestingly, the J-V characteristics remain identical after bending the device from 50–120° angles, which is attractive for flexible electronics applications.

In summary, we have synthesized S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots with average diameter 13.3nm and consist of 1–3 monolayer of graphene. XPS study confirmed the doping of S and N elements in GQDs. UV–visible absorption spectrum of S,N-GQDs shows two major peaks at 336nm and 621nm, which are attributed n→П⁎ transition in CO and SO bonds, respectively. S,N-GQDs are highly luminescent and showed excitation dependent emission behaviors. TEM, UV–visible spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy study confirmed the attachment of S,N-GQDs on nanorod surface. S,N-GQD sensitized ZnO NRs based hybrid device demonstrated higher photoresponse and IPCE value from the control samples. Such superior photoresponse properties and larger IPCE value over broad spectral range (300–700nm) is attributed to strong photoabsorption and favorable band alignment at S,N-GQD/ZnO interface. The photoresponsitivity and photodetectivity of S,N-GQD/ZnO NRs based hybrid devices under different bias voltage have been demonstrated. All hybrid devices are flexible and the current-voltage characteristics remain identical after bending the devices from 50° to 120° angles. The present study demonstrates the potential use of graphene quantum dots as a green sensitizer for ZnO nanorod/polymer hybrid photosensing devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This present research work was funded by CSIR Extramural Research Scheme, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledge the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for UV–VIS-NIR and AFM characterizations.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals of zinc oxide as a semiconductor
CdS decorated ZnO nanorod heterostructures for improved hybrid photovoltaic devices
Recent advances in ZnO materials and devices
Heterostructures of ZnO nanorods with various one-dimensional nanostructures
Thin film semiconductor deposition on free-standing ZnO columns
Electrochemical/chemical synthesis of highly-oriented singlecrystal ZnO nanotube arrays on transparent conductive substrates
Room-temperature lasing observed from ZnO nanocolumns grown by aqueous solution deposition
ZnO nanowire UV photodetectors with high internal gain
Field emission from well-aligned zinc oxide nanowires grown at low temperature
Ordered nanowire array blue/near-UV light emitting diodes
Nanoforest of hydrothermally grown hierarchical ZnO nanowires for a high efficiency dye-sensitized solar cell
CdTe quantum dots encapsulated ZnO nanorods for highly efficient photoelectrochemical degradation of phenols
Enhanced photoresponse in ZnO nanowires decorated with CdTe quantum dot
Photosensitization of ZnO nanowires with CdSe quantum dots for photovoltaic devices
